identity.short_name: Cyclic adjacent-sum squares under majorization cap

problem_spec.summary: Optimize a cyclic sum of a convex kernel applied to adjacent sums by combining rearrangement on the cyclic order with majorization/Schur-convexity under a fixed-sum plus cap constraint.
givens: Variables satisfy $0\le x_i\le \frac{2}{5}$ for all $i$ and $\sum_{i=1}^5 x_i=1$.
For a permutation $\pi$ of $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ define $S(\pi)=\sum_{i=1}^5(x_{\pi(i)}+x_{\pi(i+1)})^2$ with $\pi(6)=\pi(1)$.
Let $K=\max S(\pi)$ over all admissible $(x_1,\dots,x_5)$ and permutations $\pi$.
Write $K=\frac{p}{q}$ in lowest terms and define $N\in\{0,\dots,999\}$ by $N\equiv p+q\pmod{1000}$.
ask: Determine the exact value of $K$ and all equality cases.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: Controls dimension for n_variable inequalities and cyclic rearrangement structure.

parameter(s).name: constraint_type
parameter(s).purpose: Chooses the constrained_domain on which optimization occurs.

parameter(s).name: convex_kernel
parameter(s).domain: a specified convex/concave function $\phi$ with decidable properties
parameter(s).purpose: Allows Jensen/Karamata style reductions and changes which inequality_chain_design applies.

invariant(s).name: majorization_order
invariant(s).statement: Under fixed_sum and box-cap constraints, iterated mixing_variables produces a vector that majorizes the original while preserving the sum; Schur-convex objectives increase along this process, forcing an extremal two-level boundary vector.

invariant(s).name: cyclic_structure
invariant(s).statement: The target is cyclic in the chosen order of variables; rearrangement/sorting of the cyclic order is an essential part of extremal classification.

invariant(s).name: equality_characterization
invariant(s).statement: Equality occurs at a boundary two-level vector (forced by majorization under the cap) together with a rearrangement-maximizing cyclic order.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: The majorization interface quantity is the sorted partial-sum vector; it is used to compare admissible vectors to the maximal majorization vector under the cap.

essential_assumption(s): The constrained_domain $\{x\in\mathbb{R}^5: 0\le x_i\le 2/5,\sum x_i=1\}$ is compact, so the maximum exists.
The convex_kernel $\phi(t)=t^2$ is convex on the working interval $[0,4/5]$, enabling Jensen/Karamata/Schur-convex arguments.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The objective $M(x)=\max_{\pi}\sum \phi(x_{\pi(i)}+x_{\pi(i+1)})$ is symmetric and convex, hence Schur-convex. Every admissible vector is majorized by the unique cap-saturating boundary vector $(2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0)$, forcing the unique extremal value $K=M(2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0)$. A rearrangement/swap argument then certifies the maximal cyclic order and computes $K$ exactly.

technique(s).name: majorization
technique(s).purpose: Compare admissible vectors to a cap-saturating majorization maximum and transfer extremality to that boundary vector.
outputs: reduction to a finite set of boundary candidate configurations

technique(s).name: smoothing
inputs: fixed_sum vectors with a cap constraint
technique(s).purpose: Use mixing_variables to push mass to the boundary (0 or cap) consistent with Schur-convex monotonicity.

technique(s).name: Rearrangement
technique(s).purpose: Identify the cyclic order maximizing the adjacent-product contribution.
outputs: explicit extremizing permutation pattern (blocks of large values adjacent)

technique(s).name: inequality_chain_design
technique(s).purpose: Combine convexity/majorization with rearrangement to obtain a sharp global bound and equality cases.

technique(s).name: direct_derivation
inputs: local swap inequalities and fixed-multiset counting
technique(s).purpose: Compute the extremal value for the forced boundary multiset without auxiliary variables.
outputs: exact value of $K$ and explicit equality arrangements

theorem(s).name: Jensen
theorem(s).role: From convexity and symmetry, show averaging (mixing) two coordinates cannot increase the objective, establishing Schur-convexity.

theorem(s).name: Karamata
theorem(s).role: Convert the majorization relation $x\prec y$ into the inequality $M(x)\le M(y)$ for the Schur-convex objective.

theorem(s).name: Rearrangement
theorem(s).role: Justify that placing larger values adjacent yields larger cyclic adjacent-product contributions.

theorem(s).name: Chebyshev
theorem(s).role: Provide the local swap comparison $(u-v)(a-b)\ge 0$ used to monotonically improve cyclic order toward the extremizer.

theorem(s).name: Hardy_Littlewood_Polya_Schur_convexity
theorem(s).role: Bridge between convex symmetric objectives, majorization, and extremal two-level vectors.

critical_trick(s): Rewrite the cyclic objective as a maximum over permutations of a convex function; use symmetry+convexity (Jensen) to obtain Schur-convexity.
Use majorization under the cap to force a unique boundary multiset.
Use rearrangement/swap arguments to compute the extremal value for that multiset and certify equality cases.

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: Enumerate all permutations of 5 indices to compute $\max_\pi S(\pi)$ exactly for candidate extreme points.

library(s).name: fractions
library(s).reason: Exact rational arithmetic for $K=p/q$ and for exact evaluation of $S(\pi)$.

library(s).name: random
library(s).reason: Independent Monte Carlo sampling inside the feasible region for a secondary numerical check.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: Floating computations and comparisons during random sampling.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: Enumerate the extreme points (permutations of $(2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0)$) and for each compute $M(x)=\max_\pi S(\pi)$ by scanning all permutations; confirm the exact maximum $K=6/5$ and compute $N\equiv p+q\pmod{1000}$.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Randomly sample many admissible vectors with $\max x_i\le 2/5$, compute $M(x)$ by scanning all permutations using floats, and confirm $M(x)\le 6/5$ with near-attainment close to the predicted equality pattern.

equality_cases: x is any permutation of (2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0) and the cyclic order is a shift (or reversal) of (2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0)
